Short_Name: Capitation model is public at high level
Claim: New Zealand's general practice capitation model is publicly described at a high level, including its 2002 introduction and reweighting variables.
Test_or_Next_Step: Request current operational calculation workbook and rate tables.

Claim: The recent Sapere-related capitation work improves variables/weights but does not consider fundamental changes to the overall primary care funding model.
Test_or_Next_Step: Use as boundary condition for review: allocation reform versus supply-architecture reform.

Claim: The historical capitation model was substantially based on observed/historical utilisation patterns, which may embed access constraints rather than measure unconstrained need.
Test_or_Next_Step: Review Sapere report and Hauora materials; seek formula documentation through OIA.

Claim: The Ministry has limited direct access to PHO financial activity, ownership structures and business models, constraining monitoring.
Test_or_Next_Step: Collect PHO financial statements and compare with briefing.

Claim: PHO population-health/locality functions should be assessed separately from PHO payment intermediation and gateway functions.
Test_or_Next_Step: Stakeholder interviews with PHOs, providers, Māori/Pacific providers and Health NZ commissioners.

Claim: The 2020 Health and Disability System Review recommended that it should no longer be mandatory to contract primary health care through PHOs.
Test_or_Next_Step: Map which parts of the recommendation have or have not been implemented.

Claim: The 2020 Health and Disability System Review recommended Tier 1 funding be protected from diversion to other services.
Test_or_Next_Step: Compare with proposed demand-driven contact benefits, which are not a fixed ring-fence.

Claim: Health NZ is developing a National Primary Care Dataset linked to an 80% within-seven-days access target.
Test_or_Next_Step: Obtain data dictionary, reporting definitions and denominator rules.

Claim: Health NZ commissions ambulance services for Health NZ and ACC and receives performance data including response-time KPIs, demand, quality and call type.
Test_or_Next_Step: Seek treat-and-refer, hear-and-treat, non-conveyance and ED handover data.

Claim: Australia has a recent review proposing a new primary care funding architecture and considering blended funding.
Test_or_Next_Step: Compare recommendation structure with NZ contact benefits proposal.

Claim: Dominant capitation can weakly fund the marginal cost of additional clinically necessary contacts once a patient is enrolled.
Test_or_Next_Step: Quantify marginal revenue and marginal cost by contact type using practice data.

Claim: Fee-for-service/activity-sensitive payment creates a marginal payment signal for additional activity but may increase low-value volume if poorly specified.
Test_or_Next_Step: Extract evidence on volume, quality, cost and outcomes from review.

Claim: Blended funding can combine population accountability with marginal supply incentives and targeted chronic/complex care funding.
Test_or_Next_Step: Evidence map: blended model effects by outcome and setting.

Claim: Hospital demand is more politically visible and operationally urgent than dispersed upstream unmet need, creating a hospital-rescue equilibrium.
Test_or_Next_Step: Budget/time-series analysis and executive stakeholder interviews.

Claim: Delayed or unmet primary care need can shift some demand into ambulance, ED, admission and delayed-discharge pathways.
Test_or_Next_Step: Estimate pathway probabilities using linked data and literature.

Claim: ACC activity payments may stabilise primary care supply and should not be constrained without whole-of-system modelling.
Test_or_Next_Step: Practice revenue decomposition; ACC claims data; provider interviews.

Claim: Funding eligibility based on contact type, legal scope and clinical governance could expand supply beyond traditional GP-only capacity constraints.
Test_or_Next_Step: Scope-by-contact type safety and feasibility review.

Claim: Co-payments can operate as a demand signal but can also deter necessary care and worsen equity.
Test_or_Next_Step: Estimate price elasticity by group and contact type; design concessions/safety nets.

Claim: Top-tier KPIs can change management attention, but can also generate gaming or reporting burden if not linked to capacity and quality.
Test_or_Next_Step: Review target literature; interview executives; design balancing measures.

Claim: Unobserved upstream unmet need is less fundable than measured hospital pressure; linked primary/ambulance/hospital data are needed to test the map.
Test_or_Next_Step: Data linkage feasibility assessment.

Claim: Direct benefits cannot substitute for kaupapa Māori, Pacific, locality, outreach and trust functions.
Test_or_Next_Step: Independent Māori/Pacific review and Te Tiriti/equity impact assessment.

Claim: Telehealth can extend access but may substitute for local in-person supply if incentives reward remote scale without local integration.
Test_or_Next_Step: Review telehealth evidence; model rural substitution/complementarity scenarios.

Claim: A demand-driven benefits schedule may expand beneficial access, low-value activity, or both; fiscal risk depends on contact definitions, co-payments, audit and substitution from hospital.
Test_or_Next_Step: Scenario and sensitivity analysis.

Claim: The proposal removes funding-model professional constraints, not clinical safety constraints; scopes, prescribing rules and governance remain binding.
Test_or_Next_Step: Map contacts to scope, governance and prescribing rules.

Claim: PRISMA-ScR, STRESS, ODD and CHEERS provide appropriate reporting standards for evidence map, simulation model, agent-based model and economic evaluation stages.
Test_or_Next_Step: Apply standards during reporting, not only retrospectively.
